snapshot.packages/engine/plugin/skills/search-wik:
---
name: search-wikipedia
model: claude-sonnet-4-6
description: Look up a topic on Wikipedia — the general-purpose encyclopedia — and save the summary as a markdown file in the user's working folder. Use when the user explicitly names Wikipedia, or asks to look up or save general background on a topic, person, place, or historical event. Do NOT use when the user names the FamilySearch Research Wiki or "FamilySearch wiki" (use search-familysearch-wiki), wants a locality records-availability guide — what records exist for a place and where they are held (use locality-guide), or wants narrative genealogical history such as migration patterns or boundary changes (use historical-context).
allowed-tools:
  - wikipedia_search
---

# Wikipedia Lookup

*(This skill is intentionally minimal — it's the simplified reference example for new contributors. For the richer pattern with positive triggers and negative guards, see `citation/SKILL.md` or `record-extraction/SKILL.md`.)*

**Narration:** Read `researcher_profile.narration_guidance` from `research.json` and apply it as your narration style for this invocation. If absent, default to a one-line preamble per action.

A reference skill demonstrating the full plugin pipeline: calling an
MCP tool, populating a markdown template with the result, and saving
the file to disk. Copy this structure when wiring a new skill to one
of the other MCP tools.

## What to do

When the user asks to look up a topic:

1. Call the `wikipedia_search` MCP tool with the topic as the
   `query` parameter.
2. The tool returns `{ title, extract, url }`.
3. Read the template at `templates/wiki-summary.md` (relative to
   this skill directory).
4. Fill in the template — replace `{{title}}`, `{{extract}}`, and
   `{{url}}` with the corresponding fields from the tool result.
5. Save the result as `<title-slug>.md` in the user's current
   working folder. Build `<title-slug>` from the article title by:
   - lowercasing the title;
   - replacing every run of non-alphanumeric characters (spaces, commas,
     periods, apostrophes, parentheses, etc.) with a single hyphen;
   - trimming leading/trailing hyphens.

   Examples:
   - `"Albert Einstein"` → `albert-einstein`
   - `"Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania"` → `schuylkill-county-pennsylvania`
     (the comma collapses with the surrounding space into one hyphen)
   - `"O'Brien (surname)"` → `o-brien-surname`
6. Tell the user the file was created.

## Example

User: "Look up Albert Einstein"

You should:
1. Call `wikipedia_search({ query: "Albert Einstein" })`
2. Receive `{ title: "Albert Einstein", extract: "Albert Einstein was a German-born theoretical physicist...", url: "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albert_Einstein" }`
3. Fill in the template
4. Write `albert-einstein.md` to the working folder
5. Tell the user the file was created

output.text_response:
I'll look that up on Wikipedia now! Let me call the Wikipedia search tool and read the template at the same time.Now let me search Wikipedia for the Great Famine.Got the article! Now I'll fill in the template and save the file. The title "Great Famine (Ireland)" becomes the slug `great-famine-ireland`.The Wikipedia summary has been saved to **`great-famine-ireland.md`** in your working folder. Here's what the article covers:

---

**Great Famine (Ireland)** — also known as the *Great Hunger* or *Irish Potato Famine* — was a catastrophic period of **mass starvation and disease from 1845 to 1852**. Key facts relevant to your research:

- The west and south of Ireland were most severely affected (Irish-speaking regions)
- Known in Irish as **An Drochshaol** ("the hard times")
- Caused ~**1 million deaths** and **1 million+ emigrations**
- Primary emigration destinations: **United States, Britain, Canada, and Australia**
- Ireland's population fell by **20–25%**, and up to 67% in some towns

This aligns well with your ancestor emigrating **around 1850** — they would have been leaving right in the thick of it. For genealogical follow-up, you may want to consider searching:
- **Famine-era passenger lists** (ships leaving Irish ports, especially Liverpool)
- **Griffith's Valuation (1847–1864)** to locate the family in Ireland before departure
- **U.S. arrival records** for the likely destination port

Would you like me to search FamilySearch or plan out a research strategy for finding your ancestor's records?

output.text_response:
Before I build the guide, I need one key piece of information:I'll proceed with a broad time range (1800–1950) to give you the most useful overview. Let me load the reference materials and research the county now.Good, reference materials loaded. Now let me gather jurisdiction data and wiki resources simultaneously.The specialized genealogy MCP tools (place_search, wiki_search, place_collections, etc.) are not available in this environment. I'll compile the guide from my knowledge of Pennsylvania genealogical records and Schuylkill County — noting where MCP tools would normally provide live FamilySearch collection counts and repository URLs.

---

# Locality Guide: Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania (1800–1950)

## Jurisdiction Overview

| | |
|---|---|
| **Formed** | March 1, 1811, from **Berks County** and **Northampton County** |
| **County seat** | Pottsville |
| **Parent state** | Pennsylvania |
| **Population (selected years)** | ~12,000 (1820) → ~60,000 (1860) → ~150,000 (1900) → ~235,000 (1930) |
| **Economy** | Anthracite coal mining (dominant from ~1820); agriculture in outer townships; railroads; iron furnaces |
| **Ethnic composition** | Early: English, Welsh, Pennsylvania German. Mid-century: Irish (large influx, Great Famine 1845–52). Later: German, Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak, Ukrainian, Italian (1880s–1920s) |
| **Religious denominations** | German Lutheran, German Reformed, Welsh Methodist, Welsh Congregational, Roman Catholic (Irish and later Eastern European parishes), Episcopal, Presbyterian, later Polish National Catholic |
| **Key events** | Rapid coal boom (1820s+), Molly Maguires violence and trials (1860s–1877), large-scale Eastern European immigration (1880s–1920s), Great Depression mine closures |

---

## Boundary Changes

- **At formation (1811):** Created from eastern Berks County and southern Northampton County. Records for events *before 1811* are in **Berks County** (county seat: Reading) or **Northampton County** (county seat: Easton).
- **1843:** Schuylkill County ceded territory to form part of **Dauphin County** (minor adjustment).
- **Post-1811 boundaries** were otherwise stable for most research purposes.
- **Pre-1811 records:** Search Berks County deed books, orphans' court, and church records for ancestors who lived in the region before Schuylkill County was formed.

---

## Available Record Types

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)
- **Start date:** Pennsylvania began **statewide registration** of births and deaths on **January 1, 1906**. Marriage licenses required statewide from **September 1885**.
- **What exists:**
  - *Births 1906–present:* Pennsylvania State Archives and county courthouse (Register of Deeds / Vital Records office)
  - *Deaths 1906–present:* Same
  - *Marriages 1885–present:* Schuylkill County Orphans' Court / Register of Wills, Pottsville
  - *Some earlier local registration:* Scattered, not reliable pre-1885
- **Access:** Birth records <105 years old are restricted in Pennsylvania. FamilySearch has an indexed collection of Pennsylvania births 1906–1910 and deaths 1906–1963.
- **Pre-registration alternatives:** Church records (see below) are the **primary substitute** for all vital events before 1906.

### Church Records ⭐ *Critical for pre-1906 research*
- **Denominations and key communities:**
  | Denomination | Communities | Language of Records |
  |---|---|---|
  | German Lutheran & German Reformed | Schuylkill Haven, Auburn, Orwigsburg | German (early); English (later) |
  | Welsh Methodist & Congregational | Minersville, Pottsville, Tremont | English / Welsh |
  | Roman Catholic (Irish) | Pottsville, Minersville, Tamaqua; Saint Patrick's parishes from 1830s | English / Latin |
  | Roman Catholic (Eastern European) | Many mining patch towns from 1890s | Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak, Ukrainian, Latin |
  | Presbyterian / Episcopal | Pottsville, Tamaqua | English |
  | Polish National Catholic | Patch towns, founded ~1897 | Polish |
- **Record types:** Baptisms, marriages, burials, confirmations; membership lists
- **Where held:**
  - FamilySearch microfilm (many PA Lutheran and Reformed registers filmed)
  - Catholic Diocese of Allentown Archives (for Catholic parishes in Schuylkill County)
  - Individual parish offices (for unfilmed or post-film records)
  - Schuylkill County Historical Society (some deposited registers)
  - Presbyterian Historical Society, Philadelphia
- **Digitization level:** Mix of **indexed + images** (FamilySearch Lutheran/Reformed), **microfilm**, and **physical only** (Catholic, smaller congregations)

### Census Records
| Census | Availability | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1820, 1830, 1840 | **Indexed + images** (FamilySearch, Ancestry) | Name of head of household only (1820–1840) |
| 1850, 1860, 1870 | **Indexed + images** | All household members named; slave schedule N/A |
| 1880 | **Indexed + images** | Added relationship to head of household |
| 1890 | **Largely destroyed** by fire | Only veterans schedule fragments survive nationally |
| 1900, 1910, 1920 | **Indexed + images** | Rich data; 1900 includes month/year of birth |
| 1930 | **Indexed + images** | |
| 1940 | **Indexed + images** (released 2012) | |
| Pennsylvania State Census | **None** | Pennsylvania did not conduct state censuses |
- **Access:** All available free on FamilySearch; Ancestry also has full coverage.

### Probate and Court Records
- **Types:** Wills, letters of administration, guardianship papers, inventories of estates, estate accountings
- **Date range:** From county formation, 1811
- **Where held:** Schuylkill County Orphans' Court / Register of Wills, Pottsville (originals); FamilySearch microfilm has many early volumes
- **Naturalization records** (also court-held): Declarations of intention and petitions for naturalization filed in Schuylkill County Court of Common Pleas and District Court — **extremely important** for immigrant coal miners. FamilySearch has an indexed Pennsylvania naturalization collection.
- **Digitization level:** Early volumes on **microfilm** (FamilySearch); later records **physical only**

### Land Records
- **System:** Pennsylvania is a **state-land state** (metes and bounds; original grants from the Penn family / Commonwealth)
- **Types:** Deed books, mortgages, sheriff's deeds; original warrants and patents from the Pennsylvania Land Office (pre-county)
- **Date range:** Schuylkill County Recorder of Deeds from 1811; pre-1811 warrants in Berks / Northampton County deed books and in the **Pennsylvania State Archives** (land warrant files)
- **Coal land:** Mining companies held vast tracts; Reading Company and Philadelphia & Reading Coal and Iron Company records may document employees and tenants
- **Access:** Deed books on FamilySearch microfilm (early volumes); recent deeds online via county recorder website; land warrants at **Pennsylvania State Archives** (some indexed online)
- **Digitization level:** Early deeds — **microfilm**; warrants — **physical / partial index online**

### Military Records
| Conflict | Record Types | Where Held |
|---|---|---|
| War of 1812 | Service records, pension files | NARA (indexed on Fold3, Ancestry) |
| Mexican-American War (1846–48) | Service records | NARA |
| Civil War (1861–65) | Service records, pension files, PA Veterans' card file | NARA; PA State Archives; Fold3 |
| Spanish-American War | Service records | NARA |
| WWI Draft (1917–18) | Draft registration cards | NARA via Ancestry/FamilySearch — **highly useful for immigrant ID** |
| WWI Service | Service records (partially destroyed, 1973 NARA fire) | NARA |
| WWII Draft | Draft registration cards (1940–47) | NARA via Ancestry |
- **PA Veterans' Card File:** Pennsylvania State Archives holds a card file of veterans organized by county — useful for locating Civil War service records.
- **Note:** The **1973 National Personnel Records Center fire** destroyed 80% of Army discharge papers for 1912–1960 and 75% for USAF 1947–1963.

### Cemetery Records
- **Major cemeteries:** Charles Baber Cemetery (Pottsville, est. 1853); Odd Fellows Cemetery; numerous Catholic and denominational cemeteries across the county; **coal patch town cemeteries** (many small, associated with specific ethnic communities)
- **Online coverage:**
  - **FindAGrave** (findagrave.com): Extensive coverage of Schuylkill County cemeteries; many headstones photographed
  - **BillionGraves:** Additional coverage
- **Physical records:** Sexton records and burial registers vary by cemetery; many held by cemetery offices or churches
- **Digitization level:** **Indexed + images** (FindAGrave for most major cemeteries); small ethnic/patch cemeteries may be **physical only**

### Newspaper Records ⭐ *Highly valuable for this county*
- **The Miners' Journal** (Pottsville): Founded **1825** — one of the oldest continuously published newspapers in Pennsylvania. Carries obituaries, marriage notices, legal notices, mine disaster reports, labor news. **Essential** for Schuylkill County research.
- **Other papers:** Tamaqua Courier; Shenandoah Herald; Mahanoy City Record; various ethnic-language papers (Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak) for patch town communities
- **Where held:**
  - Miners' Journal historical run: **Pennsylvania State Library** (microfilm); **Chronicling America** (Library of Congress, partial digitization at chroniclingamerica.loc.gov)
  - **Newspapers.com** and **GenealogyBank**: Partial digitized runs
  - **Schuylkill County Historical Society**: Local newspaper files
- **Digitization level:** Partial **indexed + images** (Chronicling America, Newspapers.com); gaps filled by **microfilm** at state library

### Immigration / Emigration Records
- **Relevant ports:** Philadelphia (primary), Baltimore, New York (Castle Garden to 1890; Ellis Island 1892–1954)
- **Key record types:**
  - **Passenger lists** (1820s–1950s): Indexed on Ancestry and FamilySearch; Ellis Island database free at libertyellisfoundation.org
  - **Declarations of intention / naturalization petitions:** Filed in Schuylkill County courts; also in federal courts post-1906. FamilySearch has Pennsylvania naturalizations indexed.
  - **Passport applications:** For return visits to Europe — at NARA and on Ancestry/FamilySearch
- **Note:** Many Eastern European immigrants (Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak) made multiple Atlantic crossings before settling permanently; check both arrival and departure records.
- **Digitization level:** **Indexed + images** (Ellis Island, Ancestry); naturalizations — **microfilm** to **physical only** for later records

### Tax Records
- **Types:** Township-level real estate and personal property tax lists (assessed annually)
- **Date range:** From 1811 (some scattered pre-county assessments in parent counties)
- **Where held:** Schuylkill County Historical Society (many transcribed or microfilmed); Pennsylvania State Archives
- **Substitute value:** Tax lists can **substitute for missing census years** — especially useful for 1820s–1840s and to bridge the destroyed 1890 census.
- **Digitization level:** Partial **microfilm**; much is **physical only**

### Occupational Records (Coal Mining) ⭐ *Unique to this region*
- **Mine employment records:** Philadelphia & Reading Coal & Iron Company (P&RC&I), Lehigh Coal & Navigation, and other companies kept employee registers, accident records, and payroll books.
- **Mine inspector reports:** Pennsylvania Mine Inspectors filed annual reports from **1870**, listing fatal accidents by name, age, nativity, and mine. Highly valuable for immigrant miners.
- **Where held:**
  - Hagley Museum and Library (Wilmington, DE): Major repository for Reading Company and P&RC&I records
  - Pennsylvania State Archives: Mine inspector annual reports (some digitized)
  - Anthracite Heritage Museum (Scranton) and **Museum of Anthracite Mining** (Ashland, Schuylkill County)
- **Digitization level:** Mine inspector reports — **physical / partial online**; company records — **physical only**

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories
| Repository | Key Collections for Schuylkill County | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Federal census 1820–1940; PA births 1906–10; PA deaths 1906–63; PA naturalizations; church records (Lutheran, Reformed); land warrants | **Free** |
| **Ancestry** (ancestry.com) | Full federal census suite; PA vital records; Miners' Journal (partial); passenger lists; draft cards (WWI, WWII); city directories | Subscription |
| **Fold3** (fold3.com) | Civil War service records and pensions; WWII draft; military records | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** (findagrave.com) | Cemetery transcriptions and photos — wide coverage | Free |
| **Chronicling America** (chroniclingamerica.loc.gov) | Miners' Journal and other PA newspapers (partial runs) | Free |
| **Newspapers.com** | Miners' Journal and regional papers (partial) | Subscription |
| **Ellis Island Foundation** (libertyellisfoundation.org) | Arrivals 1892–1957 | Free |
| **PA State Archives online** (digitalarchives.state.pa.us) | Land warrants, some military records, mine inspector reports | Free |

### Physical Repositories
| Repository | Key Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Schuylkill County Historical Society**, 305 N. Centre St., Pottsville, PA 17901 | Local histories, newspaper files, tax records, family files, photographs, church registers | In-person; limited mail inquiry |
| **Schuylkill County Courthouse**, Pottsville | Deeds (Recorder's office), wills/probate (Register of Wills), court records, marriage licenses | In-person |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives**, 350 North Street, Harrisburg, PA 17120 | Land warrants, state vital records, military records, mine inspector reports, naturalization records | In-person; some records accessible online |
| **Pennsylvania State Library**, Harrisburg | Newspaper microfilm (Miners' Journal), PA publications | In-person |
| **Catholic Diocese of Allentown Archives** | Catholic parish registers for Schuylkill County parishes | By appointment / written request |
| **Hagley Museum and Library**, Wilmington, DE | Philadelphia & Reading Coal & Iron Company records, employee files | In-person by appointment |
| **Museum of Anthracite Mining**, Ashland, PA | Mining records, photographs, local context | In-person |
| **NARA Philadelphia** (now closed; records at NARA Mid-Atlantic, Philadelphia) | Federal court records including naturalizations, some census originals | In-person |

---

## Records NOT Online

The following records exist but are **not** fully available in any online database — do not assume they are lost:

- **Catholic parish registers** for most parishes (especially Eastern European Catholic congregations)
- **Township tax assessment books** (most years)
- **Pre-1906 county death/birth registers** (none exist as a systematic series; church records must substitute)
- **Coal company employment records** at Hagley Museum (not digitized)
- **Orphans' Court case files** beyond early volumes
- **Local ethnic-language newspapers** (Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak patch-town papers — most not digitized)
- **Sexton and burial records** for small denominational cemeteries

---

## Known Record Losses

- **1890 Federal Census:** Destroyed nationally by fire (1921) and water damage. **Substitute:** 1890 Veterans Schedule (partial survival); Pennsylvania tax lists; Miners' Journal; city directories.
- **No known Schuylkill County courthouse fire** (unlike many Pennsylvania counties). The county's records are considered relatively intact.
- **1973 NARA fire (St. Louis):** Destroyed the majority of Army and Air Force personnel records for 1912–1960, affecting WWI and WWII service records for Schuylkill County veterans.

---

## Research Tips

1. **Start with the 1906 rule.** For any ancestor event before 1906, go directly to church records — do not waste time searching for a civil birth or death certificate that was never filed.

2. **Identify the denomination first.** Ethnic background predicts denomination. Irish = Roman Catholic. Welsh = Methodist or Congregational. Pennsylvania German = Lutheran or German Reformed. Polish/Lithuanian/Slovak = Roman Catholic or Eastern-rite Catholic. Knowing this gets you to the right repository immediately.

3. **The Miners' Journal is indispensable.** From 1825 onward it covered the entire Schuylkill coal region. Check it for obituaries, accident reports, trial coverage (especially the Molly Maguires trials of 1876–77), marriage notices, and sheriff's sales.

4. **Pre-1811 research belongs in Berks County.** If your ancestor was in the region before 1811, search Berks County deed books and orphans' court (both on FamilySearch microfilm) and German Lutheran/Reformed church registers in the Berks County area.

5. **Mine inspector reports name the dead.** Pennsylvania's annual mine inspector reports (from 1870) list every fatal accident by miner's name, age, birthplace, and mine location — one of the best tools for identifying immigrant miners who left few other records.

6. **Naturalization records are rich for immigrants.** Declarations of intention and petitions filed in Schuylkill County courts often give birth date, birthplace, village of origin, and port of arrival. Check the FamilySearch Pennsylvania Naturalization index.

7. **Spelling varies dramatically.** Eastern European surnames were phonetically anglicized by census takers and mine foremen. Search using Soundex and wildcard patterns. Kowalczyk becomes Kovalchik; Šimkus becomes Simkus or Simchis.

8. **Check "patch town" records separately.** Company-owned mining towns (patches) were not always enumerated as named places in census indexes — they may appear under township names. Use Hagley and the Historical Society to identify which company owned which patch.

9. **FamilySearch Research Wiki article to read:** *Pennsylvania Vital Records*, *Schuylkill County Pennsylvania Genealogy* — search at [FamilySearch Wiki](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/) for current collection details and finding aids.

---

*Guide compiled from training knowledge of Pennsylvania genealogical records and Schuylkill County history. Collection counts and digitization status should be verified against current FamilySearch, Ancestry, and repository websites, as holdings change frequently. For a targeted research plan based on a specific ancestor question, use the **research-plan** skill next.*

output.text_response:
Here's a Python function that parses a CSV file, using the built-in `csv` module:

```python
import csv
from pathlib import Path


def parse_csv(file_path: str, delimiter: str = ",") -> list[dict]:
    """
    Parse a CSV file and return a list of dictionaries.

    Each row becomes a dict where keys are the column headers
    and values are the corresponding row values.

    Args:
        file_path: Path to the CSV file.
        delimiter: Column delimiter (default: ',').

    Returns:
        A list of dicts, one per data row.

    Raises:
        FileNotFoundError: If the file does not exist.
        ValueError: If the file is empty or has no headers.
    """
    path = Path(file_path)

    if not path.exists():
        raise FileNotFoundError(f"CSV file not found: {file_path}")

    rows = []

    with path.open(newline="", encoding="utf-8") as csv_file:
        reader = csv.DictReader(csv_file, delimiter=delimiter)

        if not reader.fieldnames:
            raise ValueError("CSV file is empty or has no headers.")

        for row in reader:
            # Strip whitespace from keys and values
            clean_row = {k.strip(): v.strip() for k, v in row.items() if k}
            rows.append(clean_row)

    return rows
```

---

### Example usage

Given a file `people.csv`:
```
name, age, city
Alice, 30, New York
Bob, 25, London
```

```python
records = parse_csv("people.csv")

for record in records:
    print(record)
# {'name': 'Alice', 'age': '30', 'city': 'New York'}
# {'name': 'Bob',   'age': '25', 'city': 'London'}
```

---

### Key design decisions

| Feature | Detail |
|---|---|
| **`csv.DictReader`** | Automatically maps headers → values, so you get named fields instead of positional indexes |
| **`newline=""`** | Required by the `csv` docs to prevent double newlines on Windows |
| **`encoding="utf-8"`** | Safe default; change to `"utf-8-sig"` if the file has a BOM (common in Excel exports) |
| **Whitespace stripping** | Handles CSVs with spaces after delimiters |
| **`Path` from `pathlib`** | More robust than raw string paths |

---

### Optional enhancements

**Type conversion** — values are always strings from the CSV; add casting if needed:
```python
record["age"] = int(record["age"])
```

**Handle missing values** — replace empty strings with `None`:
```python
clean_row = {k.strip(): (v.strip() or None) for k, v in row.items() if k}
```

**Tab-separated files (TSV)**:
```python
records = parse_csv("data.tsv", delimiter="\t")
```

Let me know if you'd like to add any specific behavior — like type inference, error-tolerant rows, or writing results back out!

I'll search the FamilySearch wiki for Italian birth records guidance. Let me start by loading the search tool and reading the template simultaneously.Now let me search the wiki!Excellent — 3 strong results. Now let me fill the template and save the file.The file **`italian-birth-records.md`** has been created. Here's a summary of what the FamilySearch wiki says:

---

### 🇮🇹 Finding Italian Birth Records — Key Guidance

**1. Civil Registration (Stato Civile) — post-1866 is your primary source**
Civil registration began under Napoleon in 1809, lapsed in many regions after 1815, and resumed nationwide in **1866** after Italian unification. From that point, **atti di nascita** (civil birth records) are the most useful resource — they record the child's name, birth date and place, plus the names, ages, occupations, and residence of **both parents**.

**2. Catholic Parish Registers — pre-1866 (and earlier)**
Before civil registration, baptismal records (*atti di battesimo*) in Catholic parish registers are the go-to source. These typically include the baptism date, child's name, parents' names, and godparents. Many Italian dioceses have registers dating back to the **late 1500s**, following the Council of Trent.

**3. Accessing the Records**
- Many digitized records are in the **FamilySearch Catalog**, searchable by *comune* (town).
- Records **less than 100 years old** are restricted under Italian privacy law — you'll need to contact the local **Ufficio dello Stato Civile** directly.

---

### Albert Einstein
**14 March 1879 – 18 April 1955**

Albert Einstein was a German-born theoretical physicist widely held to be one of the greatest and most influential scientists of all time. He is best known for developing the **theory of relativity**, and also made important contributions to **quantum mechanics**. He emigrated from Germany to the United States in 1933 and became a U.S. citizen in 1940.

🔗 [Wikipedia: Albert Einstein](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albert_Einstein)

output.text_response:
Now I'll fill in the template and save the file.The file **`schuylkill-county-pennsylvania.md`** has been created in your working folder. Here's a summary of what was found:

---

**Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania** is a county in the U.S. state of Pennsylvania. It was a major center of anthracite coal mining during the 19th and early 20th centuries, earning the region the nickname *"Coal Country."* The county seat is **Pottsville**, and the area attracted waves of **Irish, Welsh, and Eastern European immigrants** who worked in the mines.

🔗 [Wikipedia Article](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schuylkill_County,_Pennsylvania)

Now I'll fill in the template and save the file.The Wikipedia summary for **O'Brien (surname)** has been saved to `o-brien-surname.md`. Here's what the article covers:

- **Origin:** O'Brien is an Irish surname, the anglicised form of the Gaelic **Ó Briain**, meaning *"descendant of Brian."*
- **Famous connection:** Most famously associated with **Brian Boru**, the High King of Ireland who died at the **Battle of Clontarf in 1014**.
- **Prevalence:** One of the most common surnames in Ireland, and also widespread in Irish diaspora countries — the **United States, Australia, and Canada**.
